reasoning: The question requires the 2025 Total Fertility Rate (TFR) in South Korea to be higher than the 2023 baseline of 0.72. Statistics Korea's preliminary data already shows that the TFR for 2024 significantly rebounded to 0.75, reversing a nearly decade-long decline. Furthermore, marriages—the primary leading indicator for births in South Korea—surged by a massive 14.8% in 2024, mathematically locking in a large wave of births for 2025. Recent data from March 2025 confirms this trend, with January 2025 births jumping 11.6% year-over-year compared to January 2024 (the highest jump since 1981). The government officially projects the 2025 TFR will reach 0.79. Given the overwhelmingly positive structural leading indicators (marriages) and actual preliminary birth data for early 2025, there is virtually no realistic downside scenario where the 2025 TFR fails to surpass the incredibly low 0.72 bar set in 2023. P = 0.95.
